role.title: Sr. Director Consumer Data Product - Activation

pursuitEconomics.content:
Pursuit Economics Assessment

This Sr. Director role represents a high-value pursuit with favorable economics for Ili's profile and constraints. The position offers a salary range of $172K-$375K JD, placing Ili's typical expectation ($280K-$340K range based on Sr. Director benchmark) within the upper-middle band—a reasonable fit for a public company in the data activation space.

Yahoo's organizational leverage as a public media/advertising conglomerate means the Sr. Director title carries significant operational scope: leading a Data Activation team with defined mission/vision, working across Data Science + Engineering, and managing team of PMs JD. This is truly a director-level opportunity, not a stretched IC role.

Critical caveat: Ili's recent comms (3/12 with Giovanni Gardelli and Denys Kim) flagged a strategic concern. Both contacts recommended pursuing Supply-side instead—suggesting the Consumer Data Product - Activation side may face headwinds (e.g., organizational upheaval, lower strategic priority, tighter budgets). This intelligence directly conflicts with pursuit economics. The fact that two well-placed Yahoo contacts converged on this advice is not noise; it suggests real structural misalignment between the role's positioning and Yahoo's actual direction.

Recommendation: Revisit the Supply-side opportunity (if one exists) before heavy investment here. If pursuing Activation, use comms insight to calibrate negotiation leverage and understand team momentum before commitment.

existence.content:
Role Existence & Legitimacy

Status: Confirmed. This role genuinely exists within Yahoo's product organization. The Sr. Director Consumer Data Product - Activation is a real leadership position with specific responsibilities: leading Data Activation team, defining mission/vision, cross-functional work with Data Science and Engineering, enabling consumer data capabilities across Yahoo verticals JD.

Yahoo's organizational structure: As a publicly-traded digital media and advertising company EXT, Yahoo maintains significant data and advertising operations. Consumer data activation is a core business function, not a vanity project. The role sits within product leadership, reporting likely to VP Product or Chief Product Officer level.

Team context: The role involves management of product managers, collaboration with Data Science and Engineering teams JD, suggesting an established, mature function rather than a startup experiment. Data activation teams at scale typically require 8-15 PMs and 20+ engineers, making this a substantial organizational unit.

Application stage: Ili is currently at "applied" stage with "hot" tier designation, indicating the role has been submitted and is under active review. The comms with Giovanni and Denys (3/12) suggest the hiring team or internal network is actively engaged, which increases likelihood of interview progression.

No red flags on existence. This is a legitimate, substantive Sr. Director role within a major public company.

companyMarket.content:
Company Market Position & Dynamics

Yahoo's Market Context: Yahoo operates as a publicly-traded digital media and advertising company EXT with core business lines in search, email, content platforms, and programmatic advertising. The company has undergone significant transformation post-Verizon acquisition and subsequent independence (spun back to public markets as Yahoo in 2021 via Apollo Global acquisition and recapitalization).

Advertising & Data Market Dynamics: Consumer data activation is central to Yahoo's competitive position in the $500B+ global programmatic advertising market. Key competitive pressures include:

  • Privacy regulation: GDPR, CCPA, and emerging global privacy laws restrict first-party data collection and third-party cookie reliance EXT. Yahoo must build robust consent frameworks and first-party data strategies.
  • Google & Amazon dominance: Google's market share in search + display dominates; Amazon's retail media network is rapidly ascending. Yahoo's positioning requires differentiation in owned-and-operated inventory and exclusive audience data.
  • AI-driven personalization: Competitors increasingly use machine learning for micro-targeting and dynamic creative optimization. AI-driven tools and ML/AI for data activation are explicit requirements JD, indicating Yahoo recognizes this as competitive necessity.

Strategic Uncertainty (Key Finding): The fact that two Yahoo insiders (Giovanni, Denys) recommended Supply-side over Demand-side/Activation suggests Yahoo may be deprioritizing consumer-facing data product work relative to supply-side monetization. This could reflect:

  • Budget constraints pushing focus to higher-margin publisher/supply initiatives
  • Organizational consolidation where Activation may merge with or report differently
  • Technology platform shifts that de-emphasize this team's scope

This market dynamic should inform negotiation and role-clarity questions in interviews.

plausible.content:
Role Plausibility & Authenticity Check

Overall Assessment: Highly Plausible. This role exhibits authentic characteristics of a Sr. Director product leadership position at a major public ad-tech company.

Title & Scope Alignment: "Sr. Director Consumer Data Product - Activation" is credible naming. It reflects modern product org structures at scale: function (Consumer Data Product), domain (Activation), and level (Sr. Director). Not generic, not inflated—consistent with how Yahoo actually titles roles.

Experience Requirements Check: 15+ years experience, leading Data Activation team, managing team of PMs, cross-functional work with Data Science + Engineering JD is realistic for this level. Sr. Directors at public companies typically have 12-18 YoE; Ili's 15+ years exactly matches this band.

Technical Requirements Check: Key skills include audience segmentation, personalization, targeting, media data expertise, SQL, GCP JD. This is credible technical depth for a data product leader—not overly aggressive (doesn't demand ML PhD), but reflects modern data stack expectations. SQL + GCP + media data is precisely what a consumer data activation team leader would need.

Compensation Reality Check: $172K-$375K salary range JD. For a Sr. Director at a public company in SF/Bay Area or major tech hub, this range is realistic. Top end ($375K) likely applies to experienced hires or internal promotions with equity.

Red Flags Absent: No suspicious traits detected—no obvious keyword stuffing, no unrealistic "AI expert + 1 year XP" contradictions, no generic template language.

One Authenticity Note: The internal referral context (Giovanni & Denys providing guidance) adds credibility. Real employees providing real feedback on actual open roles is hallmark of authentic opportunities.

screenOut.content:
Screen-Out Risk Assessment

Overall Screen-Out Risk: Low to Moderate. Ili possesses the foundational credentials to pass initial screening, but specific technical depth areas warrant attention.

Clear Screening Passes:

  • Experience level: Ili has 15+ years as a senior product leader with background in ad tech, data products, and programmatic advertising ILI. Directly matches 15+ years requirement JD.
  • Domain expertise: Ad tech and data product background ILI aligns with media data expertise and data activation strategies JD.
  • Leadership scope: Ili's experience in cross-functional leadership and product strategy ILI matches requirement to lead Data Activation team and work with Data Science + Engineering JD.
  • Yahoo familiarity: Ili has previous tenure at Yahoo ILI—massive screening advantage. Internal knowledge of org, culture, and product landscape.

Potential Screen-Out Areas:

  • SQL Depth: JD specifies SQL JD as required skill. If Ili's profile emphasizes only product management without hands-on SQL experience, this could flag in algorithmic screening. However, Sr. Director roles typically expect fluency, not expertise—ability to read/interpret queries, not write complex analytics from scratch.
  • GCP Specific Experience: GCP JD is called out. If Ili's background is AWS or Azure-heavy without GCP exposure, this is a minor gap. GCP is learnable; the requirement likely tests "cloud data platform literacy" more than "worked with GCP for 5 years."
  • AI/ML Specificity: AI-driven tools and ML/AI for data activation JD are required. Screening will test whether Ili can speak to specific AI/ML applications in data activation (e.g., lookalike modeling, propensity scoring, dynamic segmentation). If background is more "traditional data segmentation," this gap is worth proactively addressing in application materials.

Screening Trajectory: Ili is likely to pass keyword screening (15+ YoE, ad tech, leadership) but may face follow-up questions on technical depth (SQL, GCP, AI/ML specifics) during recruiter call. Prepare concrete examples before interview.

needs.content:
Stakeholder Needs & Role Context

Primary Stakeholder Needs (Inferred from JD):

1. Data Activation Excellence & Scale
The hiring manager is seeking a proven leader who can operationalize data activation at scale. Role requires leading Data Activation team, defining mission/vision, and enabling consumer data capabilities across Yahoo verticals JD. This signals need for:

  • Clear, compelling product vision that unites the team around shared goals
  • Ability to translate business strategy (audience monetization, personalization) into product roadmap
  • Experience shipping data products that directly impact revenue or user experience

2. Cross-Functional Orchestration
Working with Data Science + Engineering teams JD is not a side task—it's core need. Data activation is inherently complex: requires alignment between product, ML engineers, data engineers, analytics. The leader must:

  • Speak both "product" and "data" languages fluently
  • Unblock cross-team dependencies and resolve technical-product disagreements
  • Build shared metrics and success criteria across teams

3. Technical Credibility in AI/ML Era
AI-driven tools and ML/AI for data activation JD are non-negotiable requirements. The team likely struggles with:

  • Adopting AI/ML techniques (lookalike modeling, propensity scoring, dynamic creative optimization)
  • Operationalizing models from prototype to production
  • Explaining model decisions to stakeholders and managing bias/fairness

The Sr. Director must drive this modernization without being a ML researcher—product sense + enough technical literacy to lead smart engineers.

4. Regulatory & Privacy Compliance
Implicit in "consumer data" activation: GDPR, CCPA, emerging global privacy laws. The leader must build compliant data practices that don't sacrifice activation performance. This is high-stakes: privacy misstep = regulatory fine or reputational damage.

5. Business Impact & Revenue Alignment
Data activation exists to drive monetization (higher-value ad placements, better targeting) or user engagement. The leader must translate activation capabilities into business outcomes: CPC, eCPM, ROI for advertisers.

Secondary Stakeholder Considerations: The role reports likely to VP Product or Chief Product Officer, meaning the Sr. Director is expected to shape strategy, not just execute. This requires ability to influence peers, present to C-suite, and own outcomes.

fit.content:
Ili's Fit to Role Requirements

Overall Fit Assessment: Strong to Excellent. Ili possesses deep alignment with core requirements, with some areas requiring targeted emphasis.

Tier 1 Fit (Exact Match):

  • Experience Depth: Ili's 15+ years as senior product leader ILI directly matches 15+ years experience requirement JD. Not marginal; Ili is exactly in the target band.
  • Domain Expertise: Background in ad tech, data products, programmatic advertising ILI is precisely the experience needed to lead consumer data activation. Programmatic advertising is the heartbeat of audience targeting and activation.
  • Leadership Model: Ili's cross-functional leadership, product strategy, and data-driven product management ILI directly supports the need to lead Data Activation team with mission/vision and work cross-functionally with Data Science + Engineering JD.
  • Company Familiarity: Ili's previous stint at Yahoo ILI is massive fit advantage. Knows org structure, product culture, key stakeholders, business context. Reduces onboarding risk dramatically.

Tier 2 Fit (Strong but Requires Proof):

  • AI/ML for Data Activation: AI-driven tools experience is an explicit requirement JD. Ili's background in data products likely touches ML (lookalike, propensity, segmentation), but fit here depends on depth of hands-on AI/ML product leadership. Should prepare 2-3 concrete examples: "Led adoption of ML-driven audience lookalike model, reducing CAC by X%" or similar.
  • Technical Credential (SQL + GCP): SQL and GCP specified JD. Ili's ad-tech background suggests SQL familiarity, but GCP specificity may be gap. Positioning: "Proficient in SQL, cloud-agnostic infrastructure design, recently evaluated/worked with GCP" covers this tactfully.
  • Audience Segmentation & Personalization: Core to activation. Ili's programmatic background should touch this, but ensure examples include first-party audience segmentation, not just third-party cookie-based targeting (which is increasingly obsolete).

Tier 3 Fit (Potential Gaps):

  • Privacy Compliance Depth: Consumer data capabilities JD implies GDPR/CCPA/privacy knowledge. If Ili's background is primarily US-focused ad tech, may lack hands-on experience with global privacy frameworks. Not disqualifying, but worth proactively addressing in interviews: "Led GDPR compliance for data activation product, implemented consent management integration."
  • Agile/Scrum Leadership: Agile/Scrum JD mentioned. Likely this is table-stakes at large tech companies, so Ili probably has this, but ensure resume/positioning mentions Agile/Scrum leadership experience explicitly.

Fit Conclusion: Ili is a strong internal candidate—nearly perfect for this role given prior Yahoo tenure. Gap areas (AI/ML specificity, GCP, privacy compliance) are learnable and not disqualifying. Focus interview prep on articulating specific, quantified examples of data product impact and AI/ML adoption success.

gaps.content:
Skill & Experience Gaps

Gap Severity Matrix:

Minor Gaps (Easily Addressed, Not Disqualifying):

  • GCP-Specific Depth: GCP is specified as required skill JD. If Ili's background emphasizes AWS or Azure, this is a minor gap. Cloud data platforms are functionally similar; the requirement tests cloud literacy more than GCP minutiae. Remediation: Spend 2-3 hours reviewing GCP data services (BigQuery, Pub/Sub, Dataflow) before interviews. Read one GCP case study. In interviews, frame as: "Cloud-agnostic infrastructure design; familiar with BigQuery for analytics."
  • AI/ML Product Leadership Depth: AI-driven tools and ML/AI for data activation are required JD. Gap here depends on Ili's prior exposure. If background is traditional product management without ML product experience, this is a remedial gap. Not disqualifying: Sr. Director doesn't need to build ML models; needs to lead engineers who do and translate ML capabilities into product. Remediation: Prepare 2-3 specific examples: "Shipped propensity model that improved relevance by X%"; "Led team through MLOps implementation"; "Designed A/B test framework for ML model comparison."
  • SQL Hands-On Fluency: SQL is required JD. If Ili has product background with some SQL exposure (queries for dashboards, data validation), this is a minor gap. Sr. Director doesn't write production SQL daily; needs fluency to collaborate with engineers and validate correctness. Remediation: Brush up on SQL basics (JOINs, aggregations, window functions) via quick course. In interviews, mention SQL experience in context of data validation and analytics.

Moderate Gaps (Likely Known, Should Address Proactively):

  • Privacy Compliance Framework Depth (GDPR/CCPA): Consumer data capabilities JD implies expertise in privacy-compliant data activation. If Ili's background is primarily US-domestic advertising, may lack hands-on experience with GDPR, CCPA, LGPD, and emerging regulations. Gap severity: Moderate. Yahoo operates globally; privacy compliance is non-negotiable. Remediation: Review recent privacy frameworks (GDPR Article 6 legal bases, CCPA consumer rights). Prepare narrative: "Led data activation compliance initiative for EU expansion, implemented consent management and data minimization principles." Demonstrating intent to learn and understanding of compliance mindset is key.

No Major/Disqualifying Gaps: Ili's 15+ years in ad tech and data products directly covers core domain knowledge, leadership scope, and business context. No fundamental skill deficits detected.

Gap Resolution Strategy: These gaps are confidence gaps, not capability gaps. Ili can address via:

  • Targeted pre-interview preparation (GCP reading, ML product examples, SQL refresh)
  • Proactive framing in interviews: "While my primary cloud experience is AWS, I've evaluated GCP for [use case] and am cloud-agnostic."
  • Leveraging Yahoo tenure: "My prior time at Yahoo gives me context on organizational structure and product priorities; I'm familiar with our privacy-first data architecture."

network.content:
Network Leverage & Relationship Strategy

Current Network Status: Already Activated (Significant).

Known Relationships:

Ili has already engaged with two high-value Yahoo insiders:

  • Giovanni Gardelli: Yahoo internal contact, spoke 3/12. Presumably in product, data, or technical leadership given role-relevant advice.
  • Denys Kim: Yahoo internal contact, spoke 3/12. Same context-appropriate guidance.

Key Finding: Both contacts recommended Supply-side over Demand-side/Activation. This is not a dismissal of Ili; it's strategic guidance based on internal knowledge. Interpretation:

  • Supply-side may be higher organizational priority, larger team, better staffing/budget
  • Activation team may face headwinds, reorganization, or deprioritization
  • Better long-term growth trajectory on Supply-side

Network Leverage Recommendation: Do NOT pursue Activation aggressively if Supply-side is available. This isn't a weakness of the Activation role; it reflects real organizational dynamics. If Ili has already engaged with Giovanni and Denys on this topic, the network signal is clear: they are steering away for good reason.

However, if Activation remains the target:

  • Leverage existing relationships: Giovanni and Denys are warm contacts. Ili could ask: "I appreciate your guidance on Supply-side. I'm continuing to pursue Activation as well—would either of you be willing to provide feedback on my fit and help with introductions to the hiring manager?" This reframes their advice as guidance, not blocking.
  • Secondary network expansion: If moving forward with Activation pursuit, Ili should ask Giovanni/Denys to introduce to: (a) Sr. Director or VP overseeing Consumer Data Product, (b) peer product leader on the Activation team, (c) data science or engineering lead reporting to this role. These conversations would validate whether Supply-side advice was "avoid this team" or "supply-side is better."
  • Reference-check conversation: In interview process, ask: "How is the Activation team positioned relative to Supply-side? What are the strategic priorities for the next 18-24 months?" This signals Ili has done due diligence and will help clarify organizational context.

Network Risk: The fact that two contacts converged on "Supply-side instead" should be taken very seriously. Don't dismiss this as generic advice. This is likely informed by real org dynamics. If Ili pursues Activation despite this guidance and discovers supply-side concerns post-hire, it could damage relationships with Giovanni and Denys (trust hit: "Why didn't you listen?").

Best Practice Going Forward: If Ili has a Supply-side opportunity, prioritize that based on network intel. If Activation is the only realistic path, get explicit clarity from hiring manager on team trajectory before committing.

interview.content:
Interview Preparation & Success Strategy

Expected Interview Flow:

Based on Sr. Director role at a public company, expect 4-5 rounds over 3-4 weeks:

  • Round 1 (Recruiter screen): 30 min. Qualification check, motivation, logistics. Low bar if passing screening.
  • Round 2 (Hiring manager conversation): 45-60 min. Deep dive on data activation strategy, leadership philosophy, cross-functional experience, AI/ML product work.
  • Round 3 (Cross-functional interviews): 2-3 panels with Data Science lead, Engineering lead, peer product leader. Testing technical credibility and collaboration.
  • Round 4 (Executive interview): 45 min with VP Product or Chief Product Officer. Strategic alignment, vision, leadership approach.

Key Interview Themes & Preparation:

Theme 1: Data Activation Vision & Product Strategy
Expected question: "Walk us through your approach to defining the mission and vision for a data activation team. How would you prioritize efforts across audience segmentation, personalization, and targeting capabilities?"
Preparation: Prepare a 3-5 minute narrative that includes: (a) How you'd audit current state (capabilities, gaps, competitive position), (b) Cross-functional input-gathering (data science, engineering, business stakeholders), (c) Concrete roadmap priorities (e.g., "Year 1 focus on first-party audience platform; Year 2 ML-driven lookalike; Year 3 real-time personalization"), (d) Success metrics (engagement lift, eCPM improvement, time-to-activate).

Theme 2: AI/ML Product Leadership
Expected question: "Tell us about a time you led adoption of an AI/ML capability in a product. What was the challenge, and how did you work with your data science and engineering teams?"
Preparation: Prepare 2 STAR examples (Situation, Task, Action, Result) of ML product work: (a) One technical example (e.g., "Shipped propensity model for audience targeting; worked with data team to design training pipeline, A/B tested model performance vs. rule-based baseline, achieved X% lift"), (b) One organizational example (e.g., "Led team through ML Ops transformation; established model monitoring, retraining cadence, stakeholder governance"). Include specific metrics and timelines.

Theme 3: Cross-Functional Leadership & Conflict Resolution
Expected question: "Describe a situation where product, data science, and engineering had conflicting priorities. How did you resolve it?"
Preparation: Prepare a narrative that shows: (a) Understanding of each team's constraints (engineering velocity, data quality, product market need), (b) Structured decision-making (metrics alignment, trade-off analysis), (c) Outcome-oriented resolution (not just "we compromised"), (d) Follow-up actions to rebuild alignment. Frame this as a learning moment, not a failure.

Theme 4: Privacy & Regulatory Compliance
Expected question: "How do you balance data activation performance with privacy compliance? Walk us through a specific example."
Preparation: Prepare a narrative that demonstrates: (a) Understanding of GDPR, CCPA, and emerging regulations, (b) Concrete example of privacy-compliant data activation (e.g., "Redesigned audience segmentation to rely on first-party signals and consent-based data; achieved XX% activation lift despite third-party cookie deprecation"), (c) Mindset: Privacy as product feature, not compliance burden. Mention specific techniques: consent management, data minimization, differential privacy.

Theme 5: Yahoo-Specific Context & Strategic Thinking
Expected question: "What do you understand about Yahoo's current data activation strategy, and where would you like to take it?"
Preparation: Ili's prior Yahoo tenure is a massive advantage here. Use it: "In my previous time at Yahoo, I saw [specific product or capability]. Today, the competitive landscape has shifted with [privacy regulation / AI adoption / new competitors]. I'd propose we [strategic direction]." Example: "Yahoo has strong owned-and-operated inventory and first-party email relationships. I'd focus on building a proprietary lookalike and propensity platform that monetizes those assets in a privacy-compliant way." Shows strategic thinking + contextualization.

Theme 6: Leadership Philosophy & Team Building
Expected question: "What's your approach to building and scaling a high-performing product team? How do you handle underperformance?"
Preparation: Prepare a narrative on team dynamics: (a) How you hire (what signals matter for data product PMs?), (b) How you develop people (mentorship, stretch projects, career paths), (c) Performance management (early feedback, coaching, clear expectations), (d) Inclusion & belonging (how do you build trust in diverse, cross-functional team?). Be honest about tradeoffs.

Interview Risks to Mitigate:

  • Network intel disconnect: If asked "Why Data Activation vs. Supply-side?", have a thoughtful answer ready: "Giovanni and Denys gave me candid perspective on both sides. I'm genuinely excited about Activation because [specific reason: team composition, strategic opportunity, technical challenge]. I'd like to understand your view on how the teams complement each other." This shows you took advice seriously but have independent conviction.
  • Technical credibility gap: If questioned on SQL, GCP, or AI/ML, own any gaps: "My hands-on experience has been primarily AWS, but I'm cloud-agnostic and familiar with GCP's data stack. Happy to dive deeper in technical collaboration sessions." Don't over-claim; do claim capability to learn quickly and lead engineers.
  • Previous Yahoo tenure: Ensure framing is forward-looking, not nostalgic: "My time at Yahoo gave me deep context on org structure and product culture. I'm excited to return at a more senior level and drive strategic impact on data activation." Not: "I want to reconnect with old friends."

proofPoints.content:
Key Proof Points & Positioning Anchors

Proof Points (To Lead With):

1. Direct Tenure at Yahoo
Ili has previous experience at Yahoo ILI. This is a massive credibility anchor. In cover letter, interviews, and comms: "I understand Yahoo's org structure, product culture, and competitive positioning from my prior tenure. I'm returning at Sr. Director level to drive strategic impact on data activation." This reduces hiring risk; you're not an outsider learning the org.

2. 15+ Years in Ad Tech & Data Products
Ili's background spans ad tech, programmatic advertising, and data products ILI. This directly aligns with 15+ years experience requirement and media data expertise JD. Positioning: "I've spent my career building data products that directly impact advertiser ROI and user engagement. At [prior company], I led [specific data activation capability] that generated $X revenue impact."

3. Cross-Functional Leadership Track Record
Ili's experience in cross-functional leadership and product strategy ILI is a proof point for leading Data Activation team and collaborating with Data Science + Engineering JD. Proof: Specific examples of shipping complex products requiring tight alignment between product, ML, and infrastructure teams. Quantify outcomes.

4. Data-Driven Product Management Mindset
Ili's background in data-driven product management ILI is a proof point for the role's emphasis on audience segmentation, personalization, targeting JD. Proof: Examples where product decisions were informed by data analysis, A/B testing, and metrics-driven frameworks.

5. AI/ML Product Leadership (Secondary Proof)**
This is an opportunity to position selectively. Prepare 1-2 specific examples of ML product work, framed as "Led team collaboration on ML capabilities," not "I built the ML model." Examples: (a) "Defined product requirements for ML-driven audience lookalike platform; shipped in 6 months; achieved XX% recall at 70% precision", (b) "Established MLOps governance framework for real-time personalization system; reduced model retraining latency from 24h to 4h."

6. Sr. Director / VP Level Readiness**
At Sr. Director level, you're expected to shape strategy, not just execute. Proof points: (a) Examples of setting product vision and roadmap direction, (b) Examples of influencing peers and leadership (cross-team alignment, executive communication), (c) Examples of team building and development, (d) Examples of handling ambiguity and driving clarity. Prepare stories that show you operate at this level.

Positioning Arc for Cover Letter & Initial Messaging:

"I'm returning to Yahoo to lead the Consumer Data Product - Activation team at a critical inflection point. My 15+ years in ad tech and programmatic advertising have given me deep expertise in audience segmentation, personalization, and data-driven monetization. At Yahoo, I want to build the industry's most capable, privacy-compliant data activation platform, leveraging first-party data, AI/ML for lookalike and propensity modeling, and seamless integration with our supply-side capabilities. I'm excited to work with exceptional Data Science and Engineering teams to drive $X impact for Yahoo and our advertisers."

Positioning Adjustments Based on Interview Stage:

  • Recruiter call: Lead with Yahoo tenure + 15+ years domain experience + Sr. Director readiness (motivation check).
  • Hiring manager: Shift to specific data activation vision, cross-functional leadership examples, AI/ML product examples.
  • Cross-functional rounds: Tailor to each interviewer (data science focus on ML product leadership; engineering focus on platform thinking + team collaboration).
  • Executive round: Strategy + vision + competitive positioning + team leadership mindset.

dealBreaker.content:
Deal-Breaker Analysis & Red Flags

Critical Deal-Breaker (High Severity):

The "Supply-Side vs. Demand-Side" Strategic Divergence
This is the elephant in the room. Giovanni Gardelli and Denys Kim both recommended pursuing Supply-side instead during conversation on 3/12 CTX. This is NOT casual feedback; this is two informed Yahoo insiders steering Ili away.

What this likely signals:

  • Activation team faces organizational headwinds: Potential restructuring, budget cuts, reduced strategic priority, or unclear reporting line. Not immediately fatal, but suggests the team may be under pressure.
  • Supply-side is higher organizational priority: Better staffing, better budget, clearer strategic alignment, faster growth trajectory. If both Activation and Supply-side roles are available, Supply-side is the safer bet.
  • Skepticism about Activation's impact or longevity: The advice to "pursue Supply-side instead" implies Giovanni and Denys don't see long-term wins in Activation, or they see it as less strategically important to Yahoo's future.

Deal-Breaker Decision Matrix:

  • If Supply-side role is available: STOP pursuing Activation. The network intel is too clear. Better to take Supply-side or explore other options entirely. Pursuing Activation against this guidance is ignoring expert counsel.
  • If Activation is the only realistic path: INVESTIGATE BEFORE COMMITMENT. In interviews, ask: (a) "How is the Activation team positioned within Yahoo's strategic roadmap for the next 18-24 months?", (b) "What are the team's biggest challenges right now?", (c) "What would success look like for this role in Year 1?", (d) "How does Activation team work with Supply-side? Are we competing for resources or complementary?" Answers to these will reveal whether the team is genuinely strong or facing headwinds.
  • If hiring manager answers sound defensive or vague: Major red flag. Healthy teams have clear narratives about strategy and impact. Vagueness suggests organizational uncertainty.

Secondary Potential Deal-Breakers:

  • Role scope is smaller than "Sr. Director" implies: If Ili learns the role is actually managing 2-3 PMs instead of "team of PMs," this is a step down, not a lateral move. In interview, ask: "How many PMs do you envision on this team?", "What does the current headcount look like?", "Are we planning to grow or optimize?"
  • Compensation band doesn't meet Ili's expectations: $172K-$375K range JD is wide. If offer comes in at $180K base (bottom quartile), this may not meet Sr. Director expectations. Clarify total comp expectations early: "I'm looking for $280K-$320K base + reasonable equity + benefits, targeted at top quartile for this level in market."
  • Organizational reporting line or structure is unclear: Sr. Director should have clear reporting to VP or Chief Product Officer, autonomy over product direction, and seat at leadership table. If reporting line is through middle management or reporting structure is unclear, this is a red flag (suggests role isn't as senior as titled).
  • Team technical depth is weak: If data science and engineering leads are junior or understaffed, this role becomes 70% organization-building and 30% product strategy. Not necessarily bad, but should be explicit: "You're rebuilding this team" vs. "You're scaling this high-performing team."

Strongest Deal-Breaker Indicator: The network intel from Giovanni and Denys. If they both said "Supply-side is better," listen to them. This isn't a soft preference; it's directional guidance from people who understand Yahoo's real dynamics. Ignoring this counsel to pursue Activation anyway is a judgment call, but should be made eyes-open.

nextSteps.content:
Recommended Next Steps & Action Plan

Immediate (This Week):

Step 1: Re-Engage with Giovanni & Denys on Supply-Side Opportunity
Message: "I appreciated your candid feedback on 3/12 about Supply-side being a stronger bet. Is there a Supply-side opportunity I should be pursuing directly? If so, could you introduce me to the hiring manager or relevant contact?"
Goal: Clarify whether Supply-side is an active opportunity. If yes, pivot immediately. If no, move to Step 2.

Step 2: Conduct "Back-Channel" Reference Check on Activation Team**
If proceeding with Activation: Ask Giovanni or Denys: "I'm moving forward with the Activation pursuit. Would you be willing to provide honest feedback on how you'd characterize the team's current state and strategic positioning?" This is not asking them to advocate; it's asking for a truthful assessment of team health, headcount, budget, and organizational trajectory.
Follow-up: Ask for introductions to (a) Data Science or Engineering lead on Activation team, (b) A peer product leader who works with Activation. These conversations will validate whether Activation is a healthy, growing function or one under pressure.

Step 3: Technical Preparation (GCP, SQL, AI/ML)**
- Dedicate 2-3 hours to GCP learning: Read Google Cloud Platform data services overview. Familiarize with BigQuery, Pub/Sub, Dataflow. (Goal: Cloud-literacy, not deep expertise.)
- SQL refresh: Spend 1-2 hours reviewing window functions, CTEs, and common analytics queries. (Goal: Comfortable discussing data validation and analytics logic.)
- AI/ML product examples: Document 2-3 specific examples of ML product work or adoption. Format as STAR: Situation, Task, Action, Result. Include metrics.

If Recruiter Reaches Out (Next 1-2 Weeks):

  • Confirm motivation: "I'm genuinely excited about the Activation opportunity. I have prior experience at Yahoo, deep expertise in ad tech and data products, and I'm eager to lead the team toward [specific vision—e.g., 'privacy-first, AI-driven audience platform']."
  • Ask clarifying questions: "Can you share more about the team's current state, size, and strategic priorities for the next 18-24 months?" (Signals you're doing due diligence.)
  • Confirm interview timeline and process: Understand the 4-5 round progression and timeline. Ask: "What should I prepare or review before our first conversation?"

In Hiring Manager Conversation (Likely Week 2):

  • Lead with vision: "Here's how I'd think about the Activation team's mission and roadmap over the next 18-24 months..." (Show strategic thinking.)
  • Ask about team dynamics: "What are the biggest challenges the team faces today? Where are we overextended?" (Signals you're thinking about team health and priorities.)
  • Ask about strategic context: "How does Activation position relative to Supply-side? Are we building complementary capabilities or operating independently?" (This addresses the elephant in the room indirectly. Good answer = team is secure. Defensive/vague answer = red flag.)
  • Probe on organizational support: "What level of investment and support is Yahoo committing to this team over the next 12 months?" (Gauges organizational commitment.)

During Cross-Functional Interviews (Weeks 2-3):

  • With Data Science lead: Demonstrate AI/ML product acumen. Ask: "What are the most challenging ML problems you're solving for data activation right now? How are you thinking about model governance and real-time inference?"
  • With Engineering lead: Demonstrate platform thinking. Ask: "What's the technical architecture for our audience activation pipeline? Where are the scaling bottlenecks?"
  • With peer product leader: Demonstrate cross-team alignment. Ask: "How do you see Activation supporting your product roadmap? Where could we improve collaboration?"

Before Accepting an Offer (Final Decision):

  • Confirm team size and headcount plan: "What's the current PM and engineering headcount? What's the plan for Year 1?"
  • Clarify reporting line: "Who do I report to? What's the weekly/monthly cadence?" (Should be VP+ level, regular touchpoints.)
  • Confirm compensation expectations: If offer is low: "I appreciate the offer. My expected range for this Sr. Director role is $280K-$320K base. Can we revisit?"
  • Ask references about team health: Ask Giovanni and Denys: "If I take this role, what advice would you give me about working effectively with the team and org?" (Final gut-check.)
  • Trust your instincts: If the role feels defensive, the team sounds disorganized, or the strategic narrative is unclear, consider declining. Do not accept a role just because it's offered if the fundamentals don't feel right.

If You Decide Not to Pursue Activation:

  • Reach back to Giovanni and Denys: "Thanks again for the advice. I'm pivoting focus toward Supply-side opportunities. Can you connect me with the right contact there?"
  • Explore other Sr. Director/VP roles at Yahoo or competitors (Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Snap, TikTok) where data activation or ad tech leadership is on stronger footing.

Decision Deadline: Make the "pursue Activation vs. pivot to Supply-side" decision within the next 5 business days. If you're going to pursue Activation, do it decisively. If you're going to pivot, do it quickly before time and credibility are spent on the wrong opportunity.
